topic: Someone discovers their teenager has a secret social media account
raw_output:
[story 1]

The laptop screen glowed in the dim living room, casting a cold, blue light across Maya’s face. She’d been looking for a misplaced textbook, but instead, her eyes snagged on a notification pop-up. Curiosity, sharp and unwelcome, pulled her toward the unfamiliar handle. Her fingers hovered over the mouse, a strange knot tightening in her stomach. It was a profile picture of her son, Leo, but the smile looked… different. A flicker of something cold brushed across her skin. Hesitantly, she clicked. The feed was filled with curated images—glamorous parties, cryptic captions, and interactions with people she didn't recognize. A sudden, heavy silence descended in the room. Maya leaned back in her chair, her breath catching in her throat, her hands gripping the arms of the sofa so tightly her knuckles turned white.

[story 2]

I found the login details tucked inside a seemingly innocuous folder on my son’s tablet. It was a small, hidden folder, the kind you only find when you’re searching for something specific. When I finally navigated to the profile, the sheer volume of activity was staggering. It wasn't just pictures; it was a whole persona, a carefully constructed world separate from the boy I knew. My jaw went slack. I kept glancing at the clock, a frantic need to find an exit overriding everything else. Every time I thought about confronting him, a wave of paralyzing dread washed over me, making my palms slick with sweat. I stood up abruptly, pacing the length of the rug, my movements jerky and too fast for my own comfort.

[story 3]

It started as a simple password attempt, a momentary lapse in judgment that spiraled quickly into something far more complex. When the account loaded, the atmosphere in the kitchen seemed to drop several degrees. I watched the stream of content scroll by, a relentless parade of curated perfection that felt alien and deeply private. A tremor ran through my hands as I realized the depth of the secrecy. I tried to rationalize it—maybe it was just a phase, maybe it was harmless—but the reality of what I was seeing made my chest feel tight, constricted. I backed away from the desk, bumping into the counter, and stared blankly at the wall, unable to form a coherent thought or decide what to do next.

She’d been looking for a misplaced textbook, but instead, her eyes snagged on a notification pop-up. Curiosity, sharp and unwelcome, pulled her toward the unfamiliar handle. When I finally navigated to the profile, the sheer volume of activity was staggering. It wasn't just pictures; it was a whole persona, a carefully constructed world separate from the boy I knew.
